The provided context does not specify what external knowledge source or corpus REALM retrieves from, nor does it describe how this retrieved knowledge is specifically used by the model. The excerpts only mention that REALM is a Retrieval-Augmented Language Model pre-training framework that uses a learned textual knowledge retriever to augment language model pre-training algorithms, explicitly exposing the role of world knowledge rather than storing it in parameters [1][2][3].

Citations:
[1][2][3] data/2002.08909v1.pdf
questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM (Retrieval-Augmented Language Model) retrieves from an external textual knowledge source by employing a learned textual knowledge retriever integrated into its language model pre-training process [E4][E5][E6]. This retriever explicitly queries and retrieves relevant textual information during pre-training, thereby exposing the role of world knowledge rather than relying solely on knowledge embedded within the model’s parameters [E4][E5]. The retrieved external knowledge source consists of text documents, although the specific corpus or dataset used for retrieval is not detailed in the provided evidence [E6]. Thus, the model uses the retrieved textual information during pre-training to augment its language modeling capabilities with relevant world knowledge [E4][E5].

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM, which stands for Retrieval-Augmented Language Model, is a pre-training approach that enhances language model pre-training algorithms by integrating a learned textual knowledge retriever. Unlike models that embed all knowledge within their parameters, REALM explicitly incorporates external world knowledge by requiring the model to access it via retrieval mechanisms during training. This retrieval-augmented pre-training allows the model to augment its knowledge dynamically rather than solely relying on what is stored internally in its parameters [1][2][3].

Citations:  
[1] data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunk 92.0  
[2] data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunk 87.0  
[3] data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunk 88.0
questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM, which stands for Retrieval-Augmented Language Model, is a language model pre-training approach that integrates a learned textual knowledge retriever into the training process [E4][E5]. Unlike traditional language models that embed world knowledge implicitly within their parameters, REALM explicitly leverages external textual information by retrieving relevant documents during model training [E4][E7]. This retrieval-augmented language model pre-training method explicitly exposes the role of world knowledge by asking the model to query external textual resources rather than relying solely on internal parameter storage [E5][E7].

Pre-training We pre-train for 200k steps on 64 Google
Cloud TPUs, with a batch size of 512 and a learning rate
of 3e-5, using BERT’s default optimizer. The document
embedding step for the MIPS index is parallelized over 16
TPUs. For each example, we retrieve and marginalize over
8 candidate documents, including the null document ∅.
We experiment with two choices of the pre-training corpus
X : (1) Wikipedia, which is identical to the knowledge cor-
pus Z, and (2) CC-News, our reproduction of the corpus of
English news proposed by
Liu et al. (2019).
4.4. Main results
Table 1 shows the accuracy of different approaches on the
three Open-QA datasets. REALM outperform all previous
approaches by a signiﬁcant margin. Table
1 also shows the
number of parameters for each model.
As reported in the concurrent work of Roberts et al. (2020),
the generative Open-QA systems based on T5 are surpris-
ingly powerful, with the largest T5-11B model outperform-
ing the previous best Open-QA system. Increasing the size
of T5 yields consistent improvement, but comes at signif-
icant computational cost (from Base to 11B, the model is
50 times larger, and gains roughly 5 points in accuracy). In
contrast, REALM outperforms the largest T5-11B model
while being 30 times smaller. It is also important to note
that T5 accesses additional reading comprehension data
from SQuAD during its pre-training (100,000+ examples).
Access to such data could also beneﬁt REALM, but was not
used in our experiments.
Among all systems, the most direct comparison with
REALM is ORQA (
Lee et al., 2019), where the ﬁne-tuning
setup, hyperparameters and training data are identical. Th e
improvement of REALM over ORQA is purely due to bet-
ter pre-training methods. The results also indicate that ou r
method of pre-training can be applied both on (1) the single-
corpus setting ( X = Wikipedia, Z = Wikipedia), or (2) the
separate-corpus setting ( X = CC-News, Z = Wikipedia).
Compared to other retrieval-based systems (
Asai et al.,
2019; Min et al. , 2019a;b) which often retrieve from 20 to
80 documents, our system gets the overall best performance
while only retrieving 5 documents.

Encoder or Retriever We ﬁrst aim to determine whether
REALM pre-training improves the retriever or the encoder,
or both. To do so, we can reset the parameters of either
the retriever or the encoder to their baseline state before
REALM pre-training, and feed that into ﬁne-tuning. Reset-
ting both the retriever and encoder reduces the system to
our main baseline, ORQA. We ﬁnd that both the encoder
and retriever beneﬁt from REALM training separately, but
the best result requires both components acting in unison.
Masking scheme We compare our salient span masking
scheme (Section
3.4) with (1) random token masking in-
troduced in BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018) and (2) random
span masking proposed by SpanBERT ( Joshi et al. , 2019).
While such salient span masking has not been shown to
be impactful in previous work with standard BERT train-
ing (
Joshi et al. , 2019), it is crucial for REALM. Intuitively,
the latent variable learning relies heavily on the utility of re-
trieval and is therefore more sensitive to a consistent lear n-
ing signal.
MIPS index refresh rate During pre-training, we run a
parallel process to re-embed corpus documents and rebuild
the MIPS index. This results in one index refresh per ap-
proximately 500 training steps. To demonstrate the impor-
tance of frequent index refreshes, we compare against using
a slower refresh rate. The results in Table
2 suggests that
a stale index can hurt model training, and further reducing
this staleness could offer better optimization.
Examples of retrieved documents Table
3 shows an
example of the REALM masked language model predic-
tion. In this example, “ Fermat” is the correct word, and
REALM (row (c)) gives the word a much high probability
compared to the BERT model (row (a)). Since REALM
manages to retrieve some documents with a related fact
(row (b)), the marginalized probability of the correct an-
swer dramatically increases. This shows that REALM is
able to retrieve document to ﬁll in the masked word even
though it is trained with unsupervised text only.
5. Discussion and Related Work
We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-
tences ( Kiros et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018), and para-
graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.
Retrieve-and-edit with learned retrieval In order to
better explain the variance in the input text and en-
able controllable generation,
Guu et al. (2018) proposed
a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with
high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity.

What does the retriever learn? Since the knowledge re-
trieval of REALM is latent, it is not obvious how the train-
ing objective encourages meaningful retrievals. Here, we
show how it rewards retrievals that improve prediction ac-
curacy.
For a given query x and document z, recall that f (x, z) is
the “relevance score” that the knowledge retriever assigns
to document z. We can see how a single step of gradient
descent during REALM pre-training alters this score by an-
alyzing the gradient with respect to the parameters of the
knowledge retriever, θ:

For each document z, the gradient encourages the retriever
to change the score f (x, z) by r(z) — increasing if r(z)
is positive, and decreasing if negative. The multiplier r(z)
is positive if and only if p(y |z, x) > p (y |x). The term
p(y |z, x) is the probability of predicting the correct output
y when using document z. The term p(y |x) is the expected
value of p(y |x, z) when randomly sampling a document
from p(z |x). Hence, document z receives a positive up-
date whenever it performs better than expected.

3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

th learned retrieval In order to
better explain the variance in the input text and en-
able controllable generation,
Guu et al. (2018) proposed
a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with
high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity. By jointly learning the re-
triever, REALM has the capacity to depend on information
beyond lexical overlap.
Scalable grounded neural memory The document in-
dex can be viewed as a memory where the keys are
the document embeddings. From this view, our work
share motivations with works such as product key mem-
ory (
Lample et al., 2019), which enables sub-linear mem-
ory access in a memory network ( Weston et al., 2014;
Graves et al., 2014; Sukhbaatar et al., 2015), allowing
these scalable memory layers to be integrated into large
language models. One main difference is that our memo-
ries are grounded—each memory is associated with a docu-
ment rather than unnamed value vectors. This level of inter-
pretability is crucial for applications like Open-QA, wher e
users would require provenance for a predicted answer to
be trustworthy.

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM differs from a standard parametric language model such as BERT in that REALM incorporates a learned textual knowledge retriever to access information explicitly, rather than storing knowledge solely within the model's parameters. This means that instead of embedding all world knowledge inside the model weights, REALM exposes the role of world knowledge by retrieving relevant information dynamically during inference [1][2][3].

questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM differs from a standard parametric language model such as BERT primarily in how and where knowledge is stored and accessed [E3]. Traditional models like BERT embed knowledge directly within their model parameters, effectively storing information in a parametric form internal to the model [E3]. In contrast, REALM explicitly augments its language model pre-training with a learned textual knowledge retriever that accesses external documents during both training and inference, rather than relying solely on stored parameters [E3]. This means that while BERT predicts masked tokens only based on its internal parameters, REALM conditions predictions on retrieved documents from a large text corpus [E2]. For example, REALM can assign a much higher probability to a correct masked token when the relevant document is retrieved—for instance, REALM assigns probability 1.0 to the token “Fermat” conditioned on a retrieved document, compared to BERT’s 1.1 × 10⁻¹⁴ without retrieval [E2].

REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training

retain knowledge from millions of documents, since a ques-
tion could be about any of them.
We focus on Open-QA systems that utilize a textual knowl-
edge corpus Z as the knowledge source. Many of these
systems employ a retrieval-based approach: given a ques-
tion x, retrieve potentially relevant documents z from
the corpus Z, and then extract an answer y from the
documents (
Brill et al., 2002; Chen et al. , 2017; Lee et al. ,
2019). Our approach, REALM, is inspired by this
paradigm and extends it to language model pre-training.
Alternatively, some recent work has proposed generation-
based systems that apply a sequence-to-sequence model on
x to directly generate y token-by-token (Lewis et al., 2019;
Raffel et al., 2019). We will compare against state-of-the-
art systems from both paradigms in our experiments.
3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-
tion
3.3, we show how to implement REALM pre-training
and ﬁne-tuning by maximizing the likelihood of REALM’s
generative process. En route, we address important compu-
tational challenges, explain why training works, and also
discuss strategies for injecting useful inductive biases. The
overall framework is illustrated in Figure
2.
3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-
eling: x is a sentence from a pre-training corpus X with
some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding
p(y |x) =
∑
z∈Z
p(y |z, x) p(z |x). (1)
3.2. Model architecture
We now describe the two key components: the
neural knowledge retriever , which models p(z |x),
and the knowledge-augmented encoder , which models
p(y |z, x).
Knowledge Retriever The retriever is deﬁned using a
dense inner product model:
p(z |x) = exp f (x, z)
∑
z′ exp f (x, z′) ,
f (x, z) = Embedinput(x)⊤ Embeddoc(z),
where Embedinput and Embeddoc are embedding functions
that map x and z respectively to d-dimensional vectors.
The relevance score f (x, z) between x and z is deﬁned as
the inner product of the vector embeddings. The retrieval
distribution is the softmax over all relevance scores.
We implement the embedding functions using BERT-style
Transformers (
Devlin et al., 2018). Following standard
practices, we join spans of text by applying wordpiece tok-
enization, separating them with [SEP] tokens, preﬁxing a
[CLS] token, and appending a ﬁnal [SEP] token.
joinBERT(x) = [CLS]x[SEP]
joinBERT(x1, x2) = [CLS]x1[SEP]x2[SEP]
As in
Devlin et al. (2018), we pass this into a Transformer,
which produces one vector for each token, including the
vector corresponding to [CLS] which is used as a “pooled”
representation of the sequence (denoted BERTCLS). Finally,
we perform a linear projection to reduce the dimensionality
of the vector, denoted as a projection matrix W:
Embedinput(x) = WinputBERTCLS(joinBERT(x))
Embeddoc(z) = WdocBERTCLS(joinBERT(ztitle, zbody))
where ztitle is the document’s title and zbody is its body.

While asynchronous refreshes can be used for both pre-

What does the retriever learn? Since the knowledge re-
trieval of REALM is latent, it is not obvious how the train-
ing objective encourages meaningful retrievals. Here, we
show how it rewards retrievals that improve prediction ac-
curacy.
For a given query x and document z, recall that f (x, z) is
the “relevance score” that the knowledge retriever assigns
to document z. We can see how a single step of gradient
descent during REALM pre-training alters this score by an-
alyzing the gradient with respect to the parameters of the
knowledge retriever, θ:

For each document z, the gradient encourages the retriever
to change the score f (x, z) by r(z) — increasing if r(z)
is positive, and decreasing if negative. The multiplier r(z)
is positive if and only if p(y |z, x) > p (y |x). The term
p(y |z, x) is the probability of predicting the correct output
y when using document z. The term p(y |x) is the expected
value of p(y |x, z) when randomly sampling a document
from p(z |x). Hence, document z receives a positive up-
date whenever it performs better than expected.
3This works because pre-training already yields a good
Embeddoc function. However, it is possible that refreshing the in-
dex would further improve performance.
3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

Thus a regular polygon with 257 sides is constructible with c ompass . . . ”)

Encoder or Retriever We ﬁrst aim to determine whether
REALM pre-training improves the retriever or the encoder,
or both. To do so, we can reset the parameters of either
the retriever or the encoder to their baseline state before
REALM pre-training, and feed that into ﬁne-tuning. Reset-
ting both the retriever and encoder reduces the system to
our main baseline, ORQA. We ﬁnd that both the encoder
and retriever beneﬁt from REALM training separately, but
the best result requires both components acting in unison.
Masking scheme We compare our salient span masking
scheme (Section
3.4) with (1) random token masking in-
troduced in BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018) and (2) random
span masking proposed by SpanBERT ( Joshi et al. , 2019).
While such salient span masking has not been shown to
be impactful in previous work with standard BERT train-
ing (
Joshi et al. , 2019), it is crucial for REALM. Intuitively,
the latent variable learning relies heavily on the utility of re-
trieval and is therefore more sensitive to a consistent lear n-
ing signal.
MIPS index refresh rate During pre-training, we run a
parallel process to re-embed corpus documents and rebuild
the MIPS index. This results in one index refresh per ap-
proximately 500 training steps. To demonstrate the impor-
tance of frequent index refreshes, we compare against using
a slower refresh rate. The results in Table
2 suggests that
a stale index can hurt model training, and further reducing
this staleness could offer better optimization.
Examples of retrieved documents Table
3 shows an
example of the REALM masked language model predic-
tion. In this example, “ Fermat” is the correct word, and
REALM (row (c)) gives the word a much high probability
compared to the BERT model (row (a)). Since REALM
manages to retrieve some documents with a related fact
(row (b)), the marginalized probability of the correct an-
swer dramatically increases. This shows that REALM is
able to retrieve document to ﬁll in the masked word even
though it is trained with unsupervised text only.
5. Discussion and Related Work
We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-
tences ( Kiros et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018), and para-
graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.
Retrieve-and-edit with learned retrieval In order to
better explain the variance in the input text and en-
able controllable generation,
Guu et al. (2018) proposed
a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with
high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity.

to the end-to-end results, we also report how often the gold
answer appears in the top-5 retrievals before applying any
ﬁne-tuning. The latter metric more signiﬁcantly isolates the
contribution of improving the retriever during pre-traini ng.

[Page 2]

decide what knowledge to retrieve and use during inference.
Before making each prediction, the language model uses
the retriever to retrieve documents
1 from a large corpus
such as Wikipedia, and then attends over those documents
to help inform its prediction. Learning this model end-to-
end requires backpropagating through a retrieval step that
considers an entire corpus of textual knowledge, as shown
in Figure
1.
The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-
ity is helpful and should be rewarded, while an un-
informative retrieval should be penalized. For exam-
ple, in Figure
1, if the model needs to ﬁll the blank
in “ the at the top of the pyramid ”, the re-
triever should be rewarded for selecting a document con-
taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear
how to adapt the retrieval mechanism: a kNN can only use
examples labeled for the target task—during ﬁne-tuning,
this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just
text, not a labeled example.
We evaluate our approach by ﬁne-tuning the mod-
els pre-trained with REALM on the task of Open-
domain Question Answering (Open-QA), one of the most
knowledge-intensive tasks in natural language process-
ing. We evaluate on three popular Open-QA bench-
marks ( NATURAL QUESTIONS -O PEN , WEB QUESTIONS , and
CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA
models, including both extremely large models that store
knowledge implicitly (such as T5) as well as previous ap-
proaches that also use a knowledge retriever to access ex-
ternal knowledge, but implement retrieval in a more heuris-
tic fashion (
Lee et al. , 2019; Min et al. , 2019a; Asai et al. ,
2019). REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on all
three benchmarks, signiﬁcantly outperforming all previou s
systems by 4-16% absolute accuracy. We also demonstrate
qualitative beneﬁts of REALM, including interpretability
and modularity.

An explicit retrieval system allows us to adapt to new

We focus on Open-QA systems that utilize a textual knowl-

systems employ a retrieval-based approach: given a ques-

documents (
Brill et al., 2002; Chen et al. , 2017; Lee et al. ,

Alternatively, some recent work has proposed generation-

Raffel et al., 2019). We will compare against state-of-the-
art systems from both paradigms in our experiments.
3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-

3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-

some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding

REALM’s retrieve-then-predict framework works by decomposing the prediction probability \( p(y|x) \) into two steps, retrieval and prediction, where \( y \) is the output (e.g., answer or masked token) and \( x \) is the input (e.g., question or masked sentence). 

1. **Retrieve Step**: Given an input \( x \), REALM retrieves relevant documents \( z \) from a knowledge corpus \( Z \). The retrieval is modeled as sampling \( z \) from the distribution \( p(z|x) \). A neural knowledge retriever parameterizes \( p(z|x) \) using a dense inner product-based similarity between embedded representations of the input and documents:
   
   \[
   p(z|x) = \frac{\exp f(x,z)}{\sum_{z' \in Z} \exp f(x,z')}, \quad f(x,z) = \text{Embedinput}(x)^\top \text{Embeddoc}(z)
   \]
   
   Here, both \(\text{Embedinput}\) and \(\text{Embeddoc}\) are embedding functions implemented by BERT-style Transformers producing fixed-dimensional vectors for \( x \) and \( z \) respectively. The retrieval distribution is a softmax over relevance scores defined by the inner product of these embeddings [1].

2. **Predict Step**: After retrieving a document \( z \), the model conditions on both \( x \) and \( z \) to predict the output \( y \), modeled by \( p(y|z,x) \).

3. **Latent Document Variable \( z \)**: The document \( z \) acts as a latent variable in the generative process because it is not directly observed during training but helps generate \( y \). The overall probability \( p(y|x) \) marginalizes over all possible documents \( z \) from the corpus:
   
   \[
   p(y|x) = \sum_{z \in Z} p(y|z,x) p(z|x)
   \]

   This means the model integrates predictions conditioned on all plausible retrieved documents weighted by their retrieval probabilities, effectively capturing uncertainty and leveraging knowledge in multiple documents [1].

4. **Training and Optimization**: REALM trains by maximizing the likelihood of this generative process. The gradient for the retriever parameters is influenced by the relative performance of each retrieved document \( z \) in helping predict \( y \):
questions.orchestrator_answer:
REALM's retrieve-then-predict framework operates by integrating a learned retriever that selects relevant documents from a large corpus, such as Wikipedia, before the language model makes each prediction [E2]. In this setup, the retriever first identifies documents that may contain useful knowledge, and then the language model attends over these retrieved documents to inform its output [E2]. 

A key conceptual component of this framework is the latent document variable \( z \), which represents the retrieved documents serving as intermediate knowledge to aid the prediction. While the evidence notes do not explicitly define \( z \), REALM formulates retrieval as a latent variable model by optimizing the marginal likelihood of a retrieve-then-predict process; this means that the retrieved documents \( z \) are not directly observed during training but are inferred through the retriever mechanism [E2]. Additionally, REALM explicitly incorporates world knowledge via a retrieval mechanism during training that connects retrieval and prediction steps, which aligns with the role of \( z \) as linking these two parts of the model [E3].

In summary, the latent variable \( z \) in REALM serves to model the retrieved documents that provide external textual knowledge conditioning the language model’s predictions within the retrieve-then-predict framework [E2][E3].

questions.baseline_answer:
The paper trains the retriever in a latent retrieval setting by optimizing the retrieval scores so that documents which improve prediction accuracy receive positive gradient updates. Specifically, for a given query \( x \) and document \( z \), the retriever assigns a relevance score \( f(x, z) \). During pre-training, the parameters \( \theta \) of the retriever are updated by the gradient of the log-probability of the correct output \( y \) given the query, expressed as

\[
\nabla \log p(y|x) = \sum_{z \in Z} r(z) \nabla f(x, z)
\]

where

\[
r(z) = \left[ \frac{p(y|z, x)}{p(y|x)} - 1 \right] p(z|x).
\]

Here, \( p(y|z, x) \) is the probability of predicting the correct output \( y \) when conditioning on document \( z \). The term \( p(y|x) \) is the expected probability of \( y \) over all documents sampled from \( p(z|x) \). This means that documents \( z \) receive a positive update when they help predict the output better than the expected performance across all retrieved documents, i.e., when \( p(y|z, x) > p(y|x) \).

This approach allows the learning signal to flow back to the retriever in a way that rewards retrievals that lead to better predictions, effectively learning to retrieve more useful documents even though the retrieval is latent (not supervised by explicit retrieval labels) [3].

As reported in the concurrent work of Roberts et al. (2020),

Before making each prediction, the language model uses

The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM employs several approximations and engineering choices to make retrieval over a large corpus practical during training and inference:

1. Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS): REALM formulates document retrieval as a MIPS problem, which enables efficient selection of the best documents from a large corpus. The retriever computation for each document is cached and asynchronously updated, reducing the computational load of considering millions of documents at each training step [2].

2. Caching and asynchronous updates: To handle the large-scale retrieval, the computation performed for each document embedding is cached and updated asynchronously. This means that the retriever does not have to recompute all embeddings in every step, which makes end-to-end training feasible [2].

3. Limiting retrieval candidates: During training, REALM retrieves and marginalizes over a limited number of candidate documents, specifically 8 candidates including a null document, rather than considering the entire corpus explicitly [1].

4. Use of a null document placeholder: Including a null document ∅ in the retrieval candidates helps in marginalizing over the possibilities efficiently during the retrieval step [1].

5. Small number of retrieval documents: Compared to other retrieval-based systems that retrieve 20 to 80 documents, REALM only retrieves 5 documents during inference, which reduces computation while still achieving strong performance [1].

6. Parallelization: To speed up the document embedding step for the MIPS index, computations are parallelized over multiple TPUs, exemplified by an embedding step spread over 16 TPUs during pre-training [1].

These strategies combined allow REALM to perform retrieval over large corpora effectively without prohibitive computational costs, even enabling the entire model to be run on a single machine with a 12GB GPU [1][2].

questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM makes retrieval over a large corpus practical during training and inference primarily by retrieving only a small number of documents—five at inference time—which is fewer than many other retrieval-based systems that typically retrieve 20 to 80 documents [E1]. It uses a learned dense retriever that is jointly trained with the language model, allowing the system to select documents that specifically help reduce the model’s perplexity rather than relying solely on lexical overlap between the query and documents; this joint training improves retrieval relevance and efficiency [E3]. Additionally, the document index in REALM functions as a scalable grounded neural memory where document embeddings serve as keys, enabling sub-linear memory access during retrieval, which contributes to scalability [E3]. Beyond these points, the provided evidence does not include detailed descriptions of other specific approximations or engineering optimizations used by REALM to speed up or scale retrieval [E1][E3].

REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
4. Experiments
We now evaluate our approach on the Open-QA task. In
this section, we describe in detail the benchmarks used and
the different approaches to which we compare empirically.
4.1. Open-QA Benchmarks
A number of benchmarks have been proposed for Open-
QA. In this work, we focus on datasets where the ques-
tion writers did not already know the answer. This yields
questions that reﬂect more realistic information-seeking
needs, and also avoids artifacts that can arise if the ques-
tion is formulated with a particular answer in mind. A
deeper justiﬁcation is given in
Lee et al. (2019). In all
cases, the predicted answer is evaluated via exact match
with any reference answer, following previous Open-QA
work (
Chen et al., 2017).
NaturalQuestions-Open The NaturalQuestions dataset
(Kwiatkowski et al., 2019) consists of naturally occurring
Google queries and their answers. Each answer also comes
with an “answer type”: following Lee et al. (2019), we only
keep questions that are categorized as “short answer type”
with at most ﬁve tokens. The dataset also provides a sug-
gested Wikipedia document to retrieve; like all models we
compare against, we do not provide this to our model.
WebQuestions The WebQuestions dataset (
Berant et al. ,
2013) was collected from the Google Suggest API, using
one seed question and expanding the set to related ques-
tions. We follow the setting deﬁned by
Chen et al. (2017).
CuratedTrec The CuratedTrec dataset is a collection of
question-answer pairs drawn from real user queries issued
on sites such as MSNSearch and AskJeeves. To account for
multiple correct answers or different spelling variations, the
answers in this dataset are deﬁned as regular expressions
that match all correct answers. It is unclear how to train
generation-based models with this type of supervision, so
we do not evaluate them on this dataset.
4.2. Approaches compared
Retrieval-based Open-QA Most existing Open-QA sys-
tems answer the input question by ﬁrst retrieving poten-
tially relevant documents from a knowledge corpus, and
then using a reading comprehension system to extract an
answer from the documents. In this paradigm, the knowl-
edge is stored explicitly in the corpus. We wish to compare
different methods for implementing retrieval.
Many approaches use non-learned heuristic retrieval such
as sparse bag-of-words matching (
Robertson et al. , 2009)
or entity linking on the question to select a small set of rel-
evant documents (e.g., 20). These documents are typically
then re-ranked using a learned model, but coverage may be
limited by the initial heuristic retrieval step. Approache s
such as DrQA (
Chen et al., 2017), HardEM ( Min et al. ,
2019a), GraphRetriever ( Min et al. , 2019b), and PathRe-
triever (Asai et al., 2019) in Table 1 are in this category.
Some recent approaches have proposed to implement learn-
able retrieval using a MIPS index. ORQA (
Lee et al. , 2019)
formulates Open-QA using a similar latent variable model
as REALM, and also trains by maximizing the marginal
likelihood. However, REALM adds a novel language
model pre-training step, and backpropagates into the MIPS
index, rather than using a ﬁxed index. In Table
1, we di-
rectly compare the two. It is also important to note that
the retrievers for both REALM pretraining and ORQA are
initialized using the Inverse Cloze Task, described in Sec-
tion
3.4.
Generation-based Open-QA An emerging alternative
approach to Open-QA is to model it as a sequence pre-
diction task: simply encode the question, and then decode
the answer token-by-token based on the encoding. While
it was initially unclear how large amounts of knowledge
could be injected into the model, GPT-2 (
Radford et al.,
2019) hinted at the possibility of directly generating an-
swers without using any given context via sequence-to-
sequence. However, their performance was not competi-
tive possibly due to the lack of ﬁne-tuning.

questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM distinguishes itself from ORQA and other open-domain QA baselines by incorporating a novel pre-training step that includes backpropagation into the Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) index, rather than using a fixed index as in ORQA [E1]. Both REALM and ORQA initialize their retrievers using the Inverse Cloze Task, but REALM's pretraining enables end-to-end learning through its retrieval mechanism, which differs from prior heuristic-based approaches [E1]. In evaluations on Open-QA benchmarks such as NaturalQuestions-Open, REALM’s effectiveness is analyzed not only by its end-to-end exact match accuracy but also by measuring how frequently the gold answer appears within the top-5 retrieved documents before fine-tuning [E2][E3]. This metric isolates the contribution of the improved retriever during REALM’s pre-training phase, highlighting the importance of retrieval quality for downstream QA performance [E2][E3]. However, the provided evidence does not include direct numerical comparisons or head-to-head performance metrics between REALM, ORQA, or other open-domain QA baselines [E1][E2][E3].

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM's retrieval-based approach differs from storing knowledge only in model parameters by making the knowledge more interpretable and modular. Instead of embedding knowledge implicitly within the model's parameters, REALM uses a learned textual knowledge retriever during pre-training. This explicitly exposes the role of world knowledge by enabling the model to access external information directly, as opposed to relying solely on what has been encoded internally during training[1][2][3].

pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -

questions.baseline_answer:
The ablation or diagnostic evidence indicating that retrieval pre-training improves REALM rather than only the reader component is provided by measuring how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals before any fine-tuning is applied. This metric isolates the contribution of improving the retriever during pre-training, as it assesses the retriever's quality independently of the reader's fine-tuning performance. Reporting this statistic in addition to the end-to-end results demonstrates the effect of retrieval pre-training on the retriever itself, rather than improvements solely coming from the reader component [1][2].

questions.orchestrator_answer: The provided evidence notes state that the Retrieval-Augmented Language Model (REALM) pre-training improves language model pre-training by integrating a learned textual knowledge retriever, explicitly exposing the role of world knowledge by prompting the model to retrieve relevant information during training rather than storing it solely in model parameters [E1][E2][E3]. However, these notes do not include any ablation or diagnostic experiments that isolate the effect of retrieval pre-training separately from improvements in the reader component alone [E3]. Therefore, direct ablation or diagnostic evidence demonstrating that retrieval pre-training itself, rather than only improvements in the reader, drives REALM’s gains is missing from the provided sources [E1][E2][E3].

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM improves open-domain question answering (QA) performance through several intertwined mechanisms involving retrieval quality, pre-training, and downstream fine-tuning.

Firstly, a critical measure of retrieval quality is how often the gold answer appears within the top-5 retrieved documents before any fine-tuning is applied. This metric isolates the retriever's effectiveness gained during pre-training, indicating that improvements in retriever quality at this stage significantly contribute to the overall system performance [1][2][3].

Pre-training enhances the retriever component so that it better identifies relevant documents containing the answer to a question, improving recall early in the pipeline. This improved retriever quality ensures that the top candidates fed into the reader component are more likely to contain the correct answer.

Downstream fine-tuning then allows the system to optimize end-to-end performance further. While pre-training strengthens the retriever in isolation, fine-tuning integrates retrieval and reading components, refining the model to answer questions more accurately by leveraging higher-quality retrieved evidence.

In summary, REALM's open-domain QA performance benefits substantially from (1) improved retrieval quality measured by the presence of the gold answer in top-k retrievals before fine-tuning, (2) pre-training that directly enhances the retriever capability, and (3) downstream fine-tuning that further optimizes end-to-end QA accuracy by adapting both retriever and reader modules together [1][2][3].

Citations:  
[1] data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunk 2446  
[2] data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunk 2447  
[3] data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunk 2444
questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM improves open-domain question answering (QA) performance significantly by enhancing the retriever component through its pre-training phase [E1]. This improvement is evidenced by measuring how frequently the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrieved documents before any fine-tuning, a metric that directly reflects retriever quality gains achieved during pre-training [E1]. This top-5 retrieval metric effectively isolates the contribution of the retriever separate from downstream fine-tuning effects, allowing clearer attribution of performance improvements to retriever enhancements [E2]. Ablation studies on the NaturalQuestions-Open dataset further show that removing critical components of REALM diminishes retrieval effectiveness, underscoring the importance of the improved retriever obtained via pre-training [E3]. Overall, REALM’s design integrates retrieval and answer prediction end-to-end, with pre-training especially focused on boosting retriever recall for relevant documents [E1][E2][E3]. The provided evidence does not detail the distinct contributions of downstream fine-tuning beyond confirming the retriever’s role, so the relative impact of fine-tuning remains uncertain based on these sources.

our main baseline, ORQA. We ﬁnd that both the encoder
and retriever beneﬁt from REALM training separately, but
the best result requires both components acting in unison.

scheme (Section
3.4) with (1) random token masking in-
troduced in BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018) and (2) random
span masking proposed by SpanBERT ( Joshi et al. , 2019).

be impactful in previous work with standard BERT train-
ing (
Joshi et al. , 2019), it is crucial for REALM. Intuitively,
the latent variable learning relies heavily on the utility of re-
trieval and is therefore more sensitive to a consistent lear n-
ing signal.

parallel process to re-embed corpus documents and rebuild
the MIPS index. This results in one index refresh per ap-
proximately 500 training steps. To demonstrate the impor-
tance of frequent index refreshes, we compare against using
a slower refresh rate. The results in Table
2 suggests that
a stale index can hurt model training, and further reducing
this staleness could offer better optimization.

example of the REALM masked language model predic-

that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-

graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.

better explain the variance in the input text and en-

a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-

high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity.

taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.

pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).

works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear

this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just

We focus on the masked language model 2 (MLM) variant
of pre-training popularized by BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018).
In its basic form, an MLM is trained to predict the miss-

pre-training corpus X (e.g., Wikipedia text), a training ex-

input x to predict the token that should go in each mask.
A good MLM must learn to encode syntactic and semantic
information (e.g., to predict “ of”) as well as some world
knowledge (e.g., to predict “ pound”).
Open-domain question answering (Open-QA) To mea-
sure a model’s ability to incorporate world knowledge, we
need a downstream task where world knowledge is criti-
cal. Perhaps one of the most knowledge-intensive tasks in
natural language processing is open-domain question an-
swering (Open-QA): given a question x such as “ What is
the currency of the UK? ”, a model must output the
correct answer string y, “pound”. The “open” part of Open-
QA refers to the fact that the model does not receive a pre-
identiﬁed document that is known to contain the answer,
unlike traditional reading comprehension (RC) tasks such
as SQuAD (

In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge

2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.

X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

A masked-language-model (MLM) pre-training objective can improve downstream open-domain question answering (Open-QA) in REALM through the following steps:

1. **Pre-training with Retrieval-Augmented MLM:**  
   REALM incorporates a retriever alongside the masked-language model. During pre-training, for each masked token prediction, REALM retrieves relevant documents from a large corpus (such as Wikipedia) to provide contextual information. This retrieval process helps the model predict masked tokens more accurately compared to standard MLM approaches like BERT that do not retrieve documents. For example, REALM assigns a substantially higher probability to correct but context-dependent tokens (e.g., “Fermat”) when relevant documents are retrieved, while BERT fails to do so without retrieval [1].

2. **End-to-End Learning of Retriever and Encoder:**  
   The retriever and encoder are trained jointly in an end-to-end fashion. The retriever is trained using a signal based on improvements in the language model's perplexity to select documents that help in predicting masked tokens. Documents that improve prediction are rewarded, while unhelpful ones are penalized. This ensures the retriever learns to select useful knowledge to reduce perplexity, thus implicitly learning what knowledge to retrieve [2].

3. **Latent Variable Formulation for Marginal Likelihood Optimization:**  
   REALM formulates the retrieve-then-predict process as a latent variable language model. It optimizes the marginal likelihood over retrieved documents, marginalizing over the top retrieved passages when predicting masked tokens. This marginalization allows the model to consider multiple retrieved documents and is critical for effective knowledge incorporation during pre-training [1].

4. **Salient Span Masking Scheme:**  
   REALM uses a masking scheme focusing on salient spans rather than random tokens or random spans (as in SpanBERT). This focused masking provides a consistent learning signal and enhances the interplay between retrieval and language modeling, making the latent variable learning more sensitive and effective [1].

5. **Improved Representations and Knowledge Encoding:**  
   Through MLM pre-training augmented by retrieval, the encoder learns to represent language with implicit incorporation of external knowledge retrieved from the corpus. This enriched representation captures important world knowledge and can generalize better to downstream tasks compared to models trained without retrieval or standard MLM training [3].

6. **Fine-tuning for Open-Domain QA:**  
   After pre-training with the retrieval-augmented MLM objective, the model can be fine-tuned on Open-QA datasets. The knowledge-enhanced representations and retrieval capability allow
questions.orchestrator_answer:
During pre-training, REALM integrates a learned retriever that selects relevant documents from a large corpus, such as Wikipedia, before predicting masked tokens [E1]. This retrieval step exposes the language model to external knowledge, as it attends over the retrieved documents to better inform its predictions [E1]. The retriever and language model are trained end-to-end using a performance-based signal derived from unsupervised text; retrievals that reduce the language model’s perplexity—i.e., improve masked token prediction accuracy—are rewarded, while unhelpful retrievals are penalized [E1]. Through this process, REALM effectively learns which documents are useful for predicting masked words without requiring explicit supervision, thereby learning to retrieve knowledge relevant to contextual prediction tasks [E1][E3].

REALM employs a salient span masking scheme during pre-training, targeting meaningful spans of text to mask rather than random tokens [E2]. This masking strategy is crucial because it enables the retriever to focus on fetching documents containing significant information needed to fill the masked spans, improving token prediction performance beyond what standard masked language models like BERT achieve without retrieval [E2]. Empirical results illustrate that REALM significantly increases the probability of correctly predicting masked tokens when conditioning on retrieved documents compared to models without retrieval, demonstrating the tangible benefit of this approach—for example, REALM assigns substantially higher probability to the correct masked word “Fermat” compared to BERT [E2].

3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

els comprehend a single document, Open-QA models must
retain knowledge from millions of documents, since a ques-
tion could be about any of them.
We focus on Open-QA systems that utilize a textual knowl-
edge corpus Z as the knowledge source. Many of these
systems employ a retrieval-based approach: given a ques-
tion x, retrieve potentially relevant documents z from
the corpus Z, and then extract an answer y from the
documents (
Brill et al., 2002; Chen et al. , 2017; Lee et al. ,
2019). Our approach, REALM, is inspired by this
paradigm and extends it to language model pre-training.
Alternatively, some recent work has proposed generation-
based systems that apply a sequence-to-sequence model on
x to directly generate y token-by-token (Lewis et al., 2019;
Raffel et al., 2019). We will compare against state-of-the-
art systems from both paradigms in our experiments.
3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-
tion
3.3, we show how to implement REALM pre-training
and ﬁne-tuning by maximizing the likelihood of REALM’s
generative process. En route, we address important compu-
tational challenges, explain why training works, and also
discuss strategies for injecting useful inductive biases. The
overall framework is illustrated in Figure
2.
3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-
eling: x is a sentence from a pre-training corpus X with
some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding
p(y |x) =
∑
z∈Z
p(y |z, x) p(z |x). (1)

We now describe the two key components: the
neural knowledge retriever , which models p(z |x),
and the knowledge-augmented encoder , which models
p(y |z, x).
Knowledge Retriever The retriever is deﬁned using a
dense inner product model:

where Embedinput and Embeddoc are embedding functions
that map x and z respectively to d-dimensional vectors.
The relevance score f (x, z) between x and z is deﬁned as
the inner product of the vector embeddings. The retrieval
distribution is the softmax over all relevance scores.
We implement the embedding functions using BERT-style
Transformers (
Devlin et al., 2018). Following standard

does not have good embeddings for Embedinput(x) and

To avoid this cold-start problem, we warm-start Embedinput
and Embeddoc using a simple training objective known as

model is trained to retrieve the document where that sen-

BERT-base model (12 layers, 768 hidden units, 12 atten-

questions.baseline_answer:
If REALM retrieves an irrelevant document, this would impact the conditional and marginal probabilities as follows:

- The probability \(p(z|x)\), which is the probability of retrieving document \(z\) given the input query \(x\), would assign some non-zero probability to this irrelevant document. This means the model is considering this irrelevant document as a possible piece of knowledge for answering the query, albeit it may not be a good match [1], [2].

- Because \(p(y|z,x)\) represents the probability of predicting the correct output \(y\) given the input \(x\) and document \(z\), if \(z\) is irrelevant, \(p(y|z,x)\) would tend to be low or close to zero, since the document does not contain useful information to correctly predict \(y\) [2].

- The final answer prediction probability \(p(y|x)\) is a marginalization over all retrieved documents weighted by their retrieval probabilities: 
  \[
  p(y|x) = \sum_z p(y|z,x) p(z|x),
  \]
  so retrieving an irrelevant document where \(p(y|z,x)\) is low will contribute little or negatively to this sum, potentially lowering the overall confidence or correctness of \(p(y|x)\) [1], [2].

Moreover, the gradient updates for the knowledge retriever will decrease the score for irrelevant documents because the gradient multiplier 
\[
r(z) = \left[p(y|z,x) \frac{1}{p(y|x)} - 1\right] p(z|x)
\]
is negative if \(p(y|z,x) < p(y|x)\). This implies that irrelevant documents, which have low \(p(y|z,x)\), will receive negative gradient updates discouraging their retrieval in future queries, thus improving the retriever over time [2].

In summary, retrieving an irrelevant document decreases \(p(y|z,x)\) for that document and can reduce the overall predicted probability \(p(y|x)\), while the retrieval probability \(p(z|x)\) will be adjusted away from the irrelevant document through training signals that encourage retrieval of more useful documents [1], [2].

Citations:
[1] Derivation of gradient and impact of documents on probabilities, REALM paper
[2] Explanation of gradient updates on retrieval scores and impact of irrelevant documents, REALM paper
questions.orchestrator_answer: The provided evidence discusses how often the gold answer appears among the top-5 documents retrieved by REALM before any fine-tuning, using this metric to isolate the retriever’s contribution during pre-training [E1][E2][E3]. However, the evidence does not explicitly define or describe the probabilities \( p(z \mid x) \), \( p(y \mid z, x) \), or how irrelevant document retrieval affects these probabilities or the final answer prediction [E1][E2][E3]. Specifically, there is no direct information on how retrieving an irrelevant document influences \( p(z \mid x) \), the conditional probability of the answer given the retrieved document and question \( p(y \mid z, x) \), or the final answer prediction outcome [E1][E2][E3]. Therefore, the evidence is insufficient to answer the question about the effects of retrieving an irrelevant document on these probabilities and on the final prediction [E1][E2][E3].

questions.baseline_answer:
When interpreting REALM's reported results, it is important to note that the evaluation includes how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals prior to any fine-tuning. This metric more specifically isolates the contribution of improving the retriever during pre-training, rather than reflecting the final end-to-end performance after fine-tuning [1][2][3]. Therefore, an understanding of these distinct evaluation phases and the limits of each metric should be considered. However, the context provided does not specify any direct comparisons to other baseline methods or limitations related to the dataset or model architecture, indicating that such missing information limits full interpretation.

Citations:  
[1][2][3] data/2002.08909v1.pdf chunks 2505.0, 2506.0, and 2507.0
questions.orchestrator_answer: When interpreting REALM’s reported results, several limitations and uncertainties should be noted. REALM’s evaluation includes not only end-to-end question answering performance but also reports how often the gold answer appears within the top-5 retrieved documents before any fine-tuning is applied [E1][E2][E3]. This “top-5 retrieval” metric is designed to more clearly isolate the contribution of improvements in the retriever component during pre-training, separate from the overall system performance [E1][E3]. However, the evidence does not explicitly discuss potential limitations of relying on this metric before fine-tuning, so it is uncertain how well this metric captures the full end-to-end effectiveness of the model after fine-tuning [E1][E2][E3]. Furthermore, the provided evidence does not include detailed quantitative comparisons between REALM and other retrieval-augmented models on the same metrics, nor does it clarify how improvements in retrieval translate into final answer accuracy relative to prior methods [E1][E2][E3]. Consequently, there remains uncertainty about how much of REALM’s reported gains derive specifically from retriever improvements versus other components or training procedures, due to the absence of comparative or ablation analyses in the available evidence [E1][E2][E3].
